text: almost Zen state, very liberating, where you can just focus entirely on one thing. Have you ever identified a bottleneck and it not been the right bottleneck? I spoke about other founders of this, which is like, I think when you're deep in the weeds in a problem, you're in this state, which I call like high stress, low anxiety, which is like, you know, you're working on a problem. It's really complicated. You don't know quite know the solution, but there's no anxiety because like, you know, that this is the thing that matters, right? The flip side is when you don't really know what it is, you're in a state where it's It's relatively low stress because you're not deep in any problem like 24 hours a day, but you're like higher anxiety because you don't know what the highest leverage point is. And I think you have to learn how to be in both ends of the spectrum. What is the rate-limiting bottleneck today? Overall, it's probably demand generation in the mid-market, if I had to guess. And actually, no, I'm not guessing. This is the bottleneck right now. the best podcasts honestly dude are when people will talk about mistakes and talk about lessons learned fewer things better what did you not do that with the benefit of hindsight you wish you'd done and on the flip side what did you do that with the benefit of hindsight you wish you hadn't done i think there was this point in 2019 2020 where we thought we could do it all like we thought we could be we're going to be great at serving small businesses we're going to be great at serving enterprise customers, greatest serving startups, and all these different customers are going to come to Brex and be totally happy with what we're offering. And I think we try to build it all. We had a small business offering. We had a startup offering. We had a mid-market offering, an enterprise offering. And I think the learning is the limiting factor in how many things it can do is not the headcount. My other team is the leadership bandwidth. It's like how much time your best people spend, you know, doing, building things that are really high quality and really high craft. That's just really hard to scale. Like you can't, it's not just a matter of adding more folks. It's like their attention being divided. And you don't trust that many people around, you know, a completely new business or a new thing off the ground. And I think that just took us we just completely underestimated how much headcount is not the bottleneck for just doing more things And I think you know a lot of the themes and mistakes that we made over the years were around that I would say Like, just like believing that we could do more than we could actually do. So I think focus was like a forced learning that we had to do over the years that I think the degree to which is true and the degree to which needs to continue being true as the company grows is something that, you know, you always think, oh, I have 100 people. I'm going to have a thousand people and do two next 10 more. And it's like, no, you're going to do maybe 30% more. Because now you have a lot more scale and you have problems of scale and you have a lot more. You can't just like build something and ship it. You have to like, you know, measure, roll it out over time. You have to have like A-B testing and a lot of things. So all these things, when you look at the complexities of doing it at larger scales, where your leadership bandwidth goes, I think it's more, way more of a baller than people anticipate. Did you hire a good leadership team? Oh, yeah. I mean, absolutely. I mean, we made a lot of mistakes at Brex. One thing we didn't get wrong and we got pretty right, in my opinion, was the people. And, you know, I think honestly, like, I think it's not an accident. We just spent a lot of time on it, like a ridiculous amount of time that most people would find, like, borderline obsessive. What did you not do that with the benefit of hindsight, you're like, fuck, we should have done that? We spend a lot of time early on focusing the founder. And I think the founder is absolutely important to Brex. And that's been like the cornerstone in which we build a company, right? We started a company to serve founders and we still serve founders exceptionally well. We launched a new banking product today, just focused on founders. But I think over time, it probably took us longer than it should have to focus on the finance team. which is like once you start scaling, you hire a finance leader. And that finance leader comes in and they have their own priorities. And I think probably I wish we were doing more for them in 2019 and 2020. And I think we, in 2021, we basically said, we're going to do that really well. So we're going to go to the most complex use case, which is like how do we serve a 10,000-person company and their financing and scale that down, which was an interesting pivot, I would say, from where we were, but I just wish we had done more of that earlier. I think one of the reasons the show has been successful more recently is because I kind of got more direct in my question asking When you look at ramps product marketing it always been tailored towards the finance teams and towards saving And I think people criticize you as saying it's more towards free things, coupons, vouchers, incentive mechanisms and not saving. Do you think that's a fair criticism? criticism. I think it is. And I actually love the contrast because I just don't think any great company was built on savings. If you go ask a CFO, if you go talk to any great CEOs and you say, hey, what moves the needle in your business? What truly made a difference in the financing? And what they're going to tell you is that what made a difference was this ability of making sure every dollar counted. How to make sure the dollars are going to the right place. And that in these places that really make a difference, then you're truly doubling down, right? You're saying, I want to understand what are the levers of growth? What are the levers of cost savings? What are the levers of where to hire? What are the levers of vendors that make a difference in your business? And it's less about cutting. It's more about shifting. That insight for us has been really remarkable because when we go talk to our customers and And we start to show them the product, how that happens on the ground, especially a complex use cases. And, you know, we go to like an enterprise company and we just show how they operate with Brex, how they would operate with Brex. And, for example, we have this feature called live budgets. Right. And what it allows you to do is to have a real time view into where your budget will land at the end of the quarter. And surprisingly enough, you'd be surprised. But if you go to any large public company and you go to the CMO and you say, hey, CMO, where are you on your budget right now? They don't have an answer. Right. And the reality is, if they knew that information, what they would do is they would say, well, I'm actually, you know, I'm a little bit ahead on this campaign. I'm spending a little bit more than I thought, but there's a lot of places that I'm not and I probably should spend more here. So this ability to reallocate funds and say, well, I'm actually going to put dollars where they convert the most is truly it truly changes the way this person thinks and operates. Because at the end of the day, you don't want to be under budget because you're leaving money on the table as a CMO. But if you go over budget, it is also horrible. So when you see the sort of the degree to which you build a product around this idea of how to make sure the dollars are going to the place where they have the highest impact and highest ROI versus just thinking about cutting that just a very different design decision that would build the whole company around and the whole product around And I think customers just see the difference Why does it resonate so well I think it's an easy pitch, right? Who doesn't like savings? But I think reality is more complicated than that. When you go down on the ground with customers and you ask them and you go to a CFO and you say, no one says no to savings. But when you say, hey, let me just tell you what we think actually matters. Let me show you when, let's talk to like a few customers here. Like call them and ask them how they run their business differently based on the kinds of things that you can do. Like when you bring all your global spend in one place and you start to see the difference of, you know, having this level of control in the software that allows you to push the dollars to the places that it matters the most and do this globally, right? And you start to add in the layers in which we do that. and I think the answer has just become different in savings and don't get me wrong, I think there is a place for that and I think offering free software and offering a product that helps to save money I think I can see why that works and I would be really surprised if we didn't see competitors in a $30 billion market to be quite honest but I just see a different reality speaking to customers. Can I stretch your thinking on marketing and ask you a question that I ask myself every day? what would Mr. Beast do if he were running Brax? I love that question. You know, I think the thing that I believe about brands is we talk a lot about this idea of like left side of the brain and right side of the brain. Right. And I think, you know, a lot of the times like, you know, I was I was talking to the team and I actually wrote an essay about the city team where, you know, we had this campaign and, you know, we ran the metrics and we're like, Hey, what is the value that Brex generates for a customer? And we learned a few things that were really interesting. So we learned that, you know, over $50 billion went through Brex. And that was automatically on budget and in policy, which is 99.9% of the spend. We also learned 11 million hours were saved by our customers using Brex. So when you look into the data and you understand, you know, what would they do before, what are they doing after? And I think the thing is, like, how do you, like, so what? Like, what does that mean, right? Well, that's my point. It's like, you know what the Brexit campaign should be then? It should be a mother picking up her child at 4 p.m. Why? Because she has that time because Brexit saved her there.
